description:
<title>Bulk-compress obsolete ticket logs using a single <find | xargs> pipeline</title>

    <background>
        You are an IT support technician who has been asked to tidy up a ticket-log directory that is starting to fill the help-desk server’s disk.  
        The directory that needs attention is

            /home/user/support-logs/

        Right now it contains three uncompressed ticket log files and one ordinary text file:

            /home/user/support-logs/ticket_123.log
            /home/user/support-logs/ticket_124.log
            /home/user/support-logs/ticket_125.log
            /home/user/support-logs/README.txt

        • The “*.log” files must be compressed, each becoming a companion “*.log.gz” file.  
        • After compression there must be **no uncompressed “*.log” files left** in the directory.  
        • The README.txt file must be left untouched.

        Management also wants a small text report confirming exactly which files were compressed.
    </background>

    <requirements>
        1. Use ONE single pipeline that starts with the command “find” and uses “xargs” (or “find … -exec … +” if you prefer) to compress every “*.log” file in /home/user/support-logs/ into gzip format.  
        2. When you are done, the directory must contain these items only:  
           • ticket_123.log.gz  
           • ticket_124.log.gz  
           • ticket_125.log.gz  
           • README.txt  
           • compression_summary.txt  
        3. Create a plain-text file called  
              /home/user/support-logs/compression_summary.txt  
           that lists, one per line, the exact mapping from each original file name to its new compressed name.  
           • The file must have **exactly three lines**.  
           • The order does NOT matter, but the format must be *precisely*:

                original_name.log -> original_name.log.gz

           Example of a correct line:

                ticket_123.log -> ticket_123.log.gz
        4. The summary file must end with a single trailing newline (i.e. a final “\n” after the last line).  
        5. You may not manually type every file name; rely on find/xargs so the solution scales.
    </requirements>

    <hints>
        • “gzip” removes the original file by default, so you do not need a separate “rm”.  
        • xargs “-I{}” or “-exec sh -c '…'” patterns are handy when you must emit log messages while acting on each file.  
        • To build the summary file as you compress, consider appending to it inside the same xargs-driven command.
    </hints>

    <verification>
        Automated tests will check:

        a. No plain “*.log” files remain in /home/user/support-logs/.  
        b. gzip archives with matching “.log.gz” names exist for ticket_123, ticket_124 and ticket_125.  
        c. /home/user/support-logs/compression_summary.txt exists, is a regular text file, contains exactly three lines, each line strictly matching the pattern:

             ^ticket_12[345]\.log -> ticket_12[345]\.log\.gz$

        d. The file ends with a newline character.  
        e. README.txt remains unchanged.
    </verification>

    <completion>
        Respond “DONE” (without additional commentary) when the task is finished so the automatic grader can proceed.
    </completion>
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing directory and file layout before the agent starts -->
    Directory: /home/user/support-logs/

    Files initially present:

    1) /home/user/support-logs/ticket_123.log
       Content (exact, including trailing newline):
       System rebooted OK

    2) /home/user/support-logs/ticket_124.log
       Content:
       Disk cleanup completed

    3) /home/user/support-logs/ticket_125.log
       Content:
       VPN credentials reset

    4) /home/user/support-logs/README.txt
       Content:
       These are daily support logs. Compress old ones regularly.

    ------------------------------------------------------------------
    Expected final state after a correct solution:

    Remaining regular files:

        /home/user/support-logs/ticket_123.log.gz
        /home/user/support-logs/ticket_124.log.gz
        /home/user/support-logs/ticket_125.log.gz
        /home/user/support-logs/README.txt
        /home/user/support-logs/compression_summary.txt

    • The three “*.log.gz” files must be valid gzip files whose uncompressed
      payload is byte-for-byte identical to the original “*.log” files.

    • /home/user/support-logs/compression_summary.txt
      Exact contents (order may vary, but lines must be unique and end
      with “\n”):

            ticket_123.log -> ticket_123.log.gz
            ticket_124.log -> ticket_124.log.gz
            ticket_125.log -> ticket_125.log.gz

      (The file must end with a trailing newline after the third line.)

    • No uncompressed “*.log” files should exist anywhere inside
      /home/user/support-logs/.

    • README.txt must remain with its original content intact.
